event: Lapita seafarers from the western Pacific reach the Tongan archipelago, marking the first human settlement of the islands.
Early Lapita settlers establish coastal villages and produce distinctive dentate-stamped pottery across Tongatapu and other islands.
Tongan society develops a powerful chiefdom, and the title of Tuʻi Tonga is traditionally said to be founded under the first holder, ʻAhoʻeitu.
The Tuʻi Tonga empire reaches its zenith, exerting influence across a maritime network spanning Fiji, Samoa, and other parts of western Polynesia.
The massive stone trilithon Haʻamonga ʻa Maui is erected near the royal centre at Heketa during the reign of Tuʻitatui.
The Tuʻi Haʻatakalaua dynasty is created to take on day-to-day governance, leaving the Tuʻi Tonga as a sacred paramount.
The Tuʻi Kanokupolu line emerges as a third major chiefly title, gradually accumulating real political power.
Dutch navigators Willem Schouten and Jacob Le Maire become the first Europeans to sight Tongan islands, reaching the northern Niuas.
Dutch explorer Abel Tasman visits Tongatapu and Haʻapai, charting the islands and trading with the inhabitants.
British explorer James Cook visits Tonga, later returning and naming them the Friendly Islands for the warm reception he received.
Missionaries of the London Missionary Society arrive but largely fail to establish Christianity amid civil unrest.
The assassination of the Tuʻi Kanokupolu plunges Tonga into decades of civil war among rival chiefs.
Wesleyan Methodist missionaries begin sustained evangelism, gradually winning converts across the islands.
Chief Taufaʻahau, the future King George Tupou I, is baptized a Christian and takes the name George.
Taufaʻahau unifies Tonga and is proclaimed King George Tupou I, founding the modern Tongan monarchy.
King George Tupou I issues the Emancipation Edict, freeing commoners from feudal obligations to chiefs.
Tonga adopts a written constitution establishing a constitutional monarchy, legislature, and protections of land tenure.
Tonga signs a treaty of friendship with Germany, gaining international recognition of its sovereignty.
Treaties of friendship are concluded with Britain, further securing Tonga's independent status.
King George Tupou I dies and is succeeded by his great-grandson George Tupou II.
Tonga signs a Treaty of Friendship with Britain, becoming a British protected state while retaining its monarchy and internal self-government.
Queen Salote Tupou III ascends the throne, beginning a long and beloved reign that strengthens Tongan culture and unity.
Queen Salote reunites the Free Wesleyan Church with a breakaway faction, helping heal religious divisions in the kingdom.
Allied forces, including New Zealand and United States troops, use Tonga as a base during the Pacific theatre of World War II.
Queen Salote charms crowds at the coronation of Queen Elizabeth II in London, raising Tonga's international profile.
Queen Salote Tupou III dies and is succeeded by her son, King Taufaʻahau Tupou IV.
Tonga regains full sovereignty and independence from British protection, becoming a fully independent kingdom within the Commonwealth.
Tonga annexes Minerva Reefs after a libertarian group attempts to establish a micronation there.
Tonga joins the South Pacific Forum and deepens regional diplomatic and economic ties.
Tonga becomes a member of the United Nations, gaining full standing in the international community.
Tonga launches the .to internet domain and pursues offshore ventures, including controversial passport and satellite-slot sales.
A six-week civil service strike pressures the government for higher pay and accelerates calls for democratic reform.
King Taufaʻahau Tupou IV dies and is succeeded by his son George Tupou V; pro-democracy riots later devastate central Nukuʻalofa.
Tonga holds its first largely democratic elections, with most parliamentary seats chosen by popular vote under reforms backed by the king.
King George Tupou V dies and is succeeded by his brother, King Tupou VI.
ʻAkilisi Pohiva, a longtime pro-democracy campaigner, becomes Tonga's first commoner prime minister after parliamentary elections.
Cyclone Gita, one of the strongest storms on record to hit Tonga, causes widespread destruction and damages the parliament building.
Prime Minister ʻAkilisi Pohiva dies in office and is succeeded by Pohiva Tuʻiʻonetoa.
The Hunga Tonga-Hunga Haʻapai volcano erupts catastrophically, triggering a Pacific-wide tsunami and severing Tonga's undersea internet cable.
Tonga, as a low-lying island state, continues to confront rising seas and extreme weather, championing climate action in international forums.
